messages.content:
I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

Compared to metal catalysts, metal oxides can further improve the CO2RR performance due to the active M-O bonds with appropriate affinity to the intermediates which could facilitate the CO2 activation and subsequently stabilize the reaction intermediates.[104] However, the relatively low stability and conductivity of various metal oxides is a problem for their application. It has been reported that construction of the metal-metal oxide interface could solve this problem to some extent, and significantly enhance the activity and selectivity of CO2RR.[105] Thus, fabrication of Bi/metal oxide heterojunctions would show some new results for high-performance CO2RR. The Bi/TiO2 heterojunction fabricated by Jia et al. shows that Bi nanoparticles with a diameter of 2.83 nm are homogeneously dispersed on TiO2 substrate.[79] The catalyst showed promising CO2RR performance owing to the most active p-band and d-band centers (Figure 9a). Li et al. synthesized a Bi/CuO heterojunction with rich oxygen vacancies through a one-pot solvothermal.[66] The catalyst displays an overall nanoribbon morphology with lengths of several micrometers and diameters of one hundred nanometer (Figure 9b). Bi nanoparticles with diameters of 0.1-0.2 nm are homogeneously dispersed on CuO substrate, indicating the single atom nature of Bi. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results show an enhanced amount of oxygen vacancies in the obtained Bi/CuO heterojunction. The synergy of Bi single atoms and oxygen vacancies together contribute to an improved CO2RR performance of catalyst. Xue et al. developed a multi-heterojunction structure achieved by Bi-Sn2Ox/CeO2 catalyst.[106] The catalyst exhibits a nanorod morphology, in which lattice fringes of diverse components can be found, including Bi, Sn, SnO2 and CeO2 (Figure 9c). The synergy of these component leads to the electron delocalization effect, which promotes the CO2 adsorption and activation as well as facilitates the formation of HCOO*. Cui et al. dopped Bi onto ZnO substrate, which leads to regulated local charge distribution of ZnO, the weakened binding energy of *H and *COOH, and increased binding energy of *OCHO.[107]

Your review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) is well-structured and covers important aspects of the topic. Below are some suggestions to polish the language and enhance clarity:

"Compared to metal catalysts, metal oxides offer the potential to further bolster CO2RR performance, primarily due to the presence of active M-O bonds. These bonds are theorized to possess an optimal affinity for reaction intermediates, thus facilitating CO2 activation and stabilizing the subsequent reaction intermediates.[104] Nonetheless, a challenge that persists with various metal oxides is their relative low stability and electrical conductivity, hindering their practical application. Recent reports suggest that constructing a metal-metal oxide interface can ameliorate these shortcomings to a degree, significantly augmenting both the activity and selectivity of CO2RR catalysts.[105]

Therefore, the engineering of Bi/metal oxide heterojunctions holds promise for unveiling advanced high-performance CO2RR catalysts. The Bi/TiO2 heterojunction reported by Jia et al. exemplifies this potential: Here, Bi nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 2.83 nm are uniformly distributed across a TiO2 substrate.[79] This catalyst showcases encouraging CO2RR performance, attributed in part to optimally active p-band and d-band centers (shown in Figure 9a). In a similar vein, Li et al. synthesized a Bi/CuO heterojunction, exhibiting plentiful oxygen vacancies, via a one-pot solvothermal approach.[66] The resulting morphology is predominantly nanoribboned, stretching over several micrometers in length and approximately one hundred nanometers in diameter, with Bi nanoparticles (0.1-0.2 nm in diameter) evenly scattered on the CuO support, reflecting the single-atom character of Bi (as illustrated in Figure 9b). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses indicate an increased density of oxygen vacancies in the resultant Bi/CuO heterojunction. It is the synergistic interplay between the Bi single atoms and these oxygen vacancies that elevates the CO2RR efficacy of the catalyst.

Building on these developments, Xue et al. introduced a multi-heterojunction architecture through the synthesis of a Bi-Sn2Ox/CeO2 catalyst.[106] The catalyst manifests a nanorod morphology, with observable lattice fringes from diverse constituent phases, such as Bi, Sn, SnO2, and CeO2 (depicted in Figure 9c). The cooperative interactions among these constituents lead to an electron delocalization effect, thereby facilitating both CO2 adsorption and activation, in addition to supporting the genesis of the HCOO* intermediate.

Lastly, Cui et al. explored the doping of Bi onto a ZnO substrate, resulting in an altered local charge distribution within the ZnO, downshifting binding energies of *H and *COOH intermediates while concurrently raising that of *OCHO.[107] Such modifications influence and potentially optimize the resultant catalytic processes."

This revision offers refined language and detailed explanations while ensuring the content remains technically accurate. It also improves the narrative flow and maintains coherence with the existing scientific literature by adhering to the citation numbers from your original text.

I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

Constructing Bi-based heterojunction with nonmetal-based materials, such as nanocarbons, MOFs, organics and doping heteroatoms, etc., is also a good choice to fabricate highly efficient catalysts because these nonmetal-based materials usually have relatively stable physical/chemical properties compared to metals or metal oxides. Nanocarbons are a kind of the most commonly used nonmetal-based supports due to their i) high electrical conductivity, ii) high specific surface area and high porosity, and iii) good interaction with supported catalysts.[108] In some cases, the carbon materials themselves can serve as promising metal-free catalysts for electrochemical CO2RR.[109,110] The integration of Bi metal with nanocarbons will alter the system’s Galvani potential, raise the electronic density in the catalysts, and lower the Fermi level, which are prone to an enhanced electrocatalytic performance.[111] Liu et al. also synthesized carbon-nanorod supported Bi nanoparticles through direct annealing of Bi-containing SU-101 MOF.[112] The fabrication of carbon support in this way facilitates the high dispersion of Bi nanoparticles and formation of rich surface oxygen, leading to optimized formation of the *OCHO intermediate. Wu et al. first modified carbon black through moderate oxidation and then used it as support for Bi nanoparticles.[53] The carbon support can provide abundant anchoring sites, appropriate surface wettability, desirable key intermediate adsorption abilities, and facilitated electron transfer abilities for Bi nanoparticles, leading to rationally modulated CO2RR performance. Using heteroatom-doped carbon will further enhance the interaction between catalyst and support and improve the CO2RR performance of Bi-based heterojunctions. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized N-doped carbon nanobundles confined Bi single atoms by annealing of Bi-MOFs.[18] The carbon support shows a highly porous feature with highly dispersed Bi single atoms in it (Figure 10a). The C K-edge X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectra clearly show a π* transition at a low energy, suggesting the formation of Bi-N coordination for Bi-N4 sites after pyrolysis of MOF, which would facilitate the formation of *COOH. Wang et al. also supporting Bi single atoms on carbon materials, but N,S-co-doped carbon (NSC) was used as support.[113] The N and S effectively co-coordinate with Bi, forming Bi-N3S sites, which can greatly reduce the energy barrier of the intermediate formation in rate-limiting step and increase the reaction kinetics. Despite single atoms, Bi catalysts with different morphology such as nanoparticle, nanosheet and nanoplate are also developed to form a heterojunction with heteroatom-doped carbon support.[80,114-116]
Using heteroatoms to dop Bi catalyst is another efficient way to construct high-performance Bi-based heterojunction. Lv et al. developed S-doped Bi nanosheets in which sulfur tends to bind to the Bi edge defects as evidenced by extended X-ray absorption fine structure spectra (EXAFS), which decreases the coordination-unsaturated Bi sites for *H adsorption and regulates the charge states of neighboring Bi sites to improve *OCHO adsorption.[117] Wang et al. also fabricated S-doped Bi catalyst.[73] The catalyst shows nanosheets morphology as evidenced by scanning electron microscope (SEM) image (Figure 10b). The doping of sulfur will lead to a co-regulation of intermediate binding and water activation, thus facilitate CO2RR. Cui et al. doped Bi nanoparticles with Te, which modifies the electronic structure of Bi by lowering the oxidation state and increasing oxygen vacancies, thus weakening the adsorption of H2O molecules and favoring the formation of intermediates.[50]
Some other nonmetal-based materials are also utilized as supports for Bi metals. For instance, Shi et al. used trimethoxy (1H,1H,2H,2Hheptadecafluorodecyl) silane (FAS) to decorated Bi nanosheets.[118] After such decoration, long fluoroalkyl chains (-C8F17), Bi-O-Si and Si-O-Si linkage are introduced on Bi surface, leading to a hydrophobic surface properties which is beneficial for the activation path by lowering thermodynamic barriers of *OCHO (Figure 10c). Takaoka et al. used Zr-based MOF (UiO-66) to form a heterojunction with Bi catalyst.[119] During the CO2RR process, CO2 molecules might be captured in a form of Zr-based carbonate due to the presence of UiO-66, which leads to a high CO2 conversion efficiency. Besides, C3N4 is also a good candidate to support Bi catalyst to optimize the electronic structure and performance of Bi.[70]

You've covered an extensive range of topics within the scope of bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction. Here's how the content can be polished for enhanced fluency and clarity:

"In the realm of nanomaterial-enhanced electrocatalysis, constructing Bi-based heterojunctions paired with nonmetal-based materials—including nanocarbons, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), organic molecules, and heteroatom dopants—has emerged as a promising strategy for fabricating highly efficient catalysts. Such nonmetal-based materials are often characterized by their robust physical and chemical stability when compared to metallic counterparts or metal oxides. Specifically, the distinct advantages of nanocarbons—namely, their high electrical conductivity, expansive specific surface area, and substantial porosity—together with their favorable interaction with supported catalysts, render them frequent choices for nonmetal-based supports.[108] Remarkably, in certain contexts, carbon materials alone have demonstrated potential as effective, metal-free catalysts for the electrochemical conversion of CO2.[109,110]

When integrated with nanocarbons, Bi metals are known to shift the system's Galvani potential, amplify the catalysts' electronic density, and depress the Fermi level, thereby setting the stage for superior electrocatalytic performance.[111] For instance, Liu et al. produced carbon nanorods adorned with Bi nanoparticles via the direct annealing of a Bi-rich SU-101 MOF.[112] This preparation technique enhances the dispersion of Bi nanoparticles and enables the formation of oxygen-rich surfaces, both of which optimize the generation of *OCHO intermediates. Wu et al. adopted a preliminary moderate oxidation of carbon black, subsequently employing it as a foundation for Bi nanoparticles.[53] The modified carbon support provides copious anchoring sites, optimized surface wettability, and desired adsorption abilities for key intermediates, all converging to facilitate electron transfer and modulate CO2RR performance strategically.

Employing heteroatom-doped carbon can further bolster the interaction between the catalyst and its support, thus enhancing the CO2RR efficiency of Bi-based heterojunctions. Zhang et al. generated N-doped carbon nanobundles confining Bi single atoms by annealing Bi-MOFs.[18] The resulting carbon matrix featured a highly porous structure with Bi atoms dispersed at the atomic level (as seen in Figure 10a). The C K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectra highlighted a π* transition indicative of low energy, signaling the emergence of Bi-N coordination at Bi-N4 sites post-pyrolysis, which facilitates the formation of *COOH intermediates. Expanding upon this approach, Wang et al. supported Bi single atoms on a carbon substrate with concurrent N and S doping.[113] The dual-doping resulted in the establishment of Bi-N3S sites, leading to a significant reduction in the energy barrier of intermediate formation within the rate-limiting step and ramping up reaction kinetics. In addition to single atoms, heterojunctions have also been developed with Bi catalysts in varied forms such as nanoparticles, nanosheets, and nanoplates, using heteroatom-doped carbon outcomes.[80,114-116]

Doping heteroatoms directly into the Bi catalyst presents an alternative, efficacious approach for conceiving high-performance Bi-based heterojunctions. Lv et al. devised S-doped Bi nanosheets, wherein sulfur preferentially associates with edge defects of Bi, as confirmed by extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra.[117] This sulfuration reduces the number of under-coordinated Bi sites and adjusts the charge states of adjacent Bi atoms, facilitating *OCHO adsorption. Furthermore, Wang et al. also produced S-doped Bi nanosheets.[73] Characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging, these nanosheets (Figure 10b) exhibit properties where the sulfur doping co-regulates intermediate binding and water activation, thus expediting CO2RR performance. Additionally, Cui et al. ventured into doping Bi nanoparticles with Te, achieving an altered electronic structure of Bi marked by lower oxidation states and an increase in oxygen vacancies. This modification attenuates the adsorption strength of H2O molecules, ultimately promoting intermediate formation.[50]

Venturing beyond traditional supports, novel nonmetal-based materials are also being explored. For example, Shi et al. applied trimethoxy (1H,1H,2H,2H-heptadecafluorodecyl) silane (FAS) to craft Bi nanosheets with unique surface chemistry.[118] Post-decoration, the presence of long fluoroalkyl chains (-C8F17), Bi-O-Si, and Si-O-Si linkages on the Bi surface imbued it with distinct hydrophobic properties, likely advantageous for the activation path by diminishing the thermodynamic barriers for *OCHO formation (refer to Figure 10c). Additionally, Takaoka et al. integrated a Zr-based MOF (UiO-66) within a heterojunction architecture coupled to a Bi catalyst.[119] During the CO2RR process, the potential capture of CO2 molecules in the form of Zr-based carbonate facilitated by UiO-66 translates to heightened CO2 conversion efficiency. Furthermore, C3N4, known for its capability to fine-tune electronic structures, has demonstrated utility when supporting Bi catalysts, aligning with the overarching goal of optimizing the efficacy of Bi-based catalysis.[70]"

I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

Bi oxides have drawn a great deal of attention for electrochemical CO2RR applications owing to their availability, low cost, structural diversity, and chemical stability and affinity to intermediates.[120] Nevertheless, it has been demonstrated that bulk oxides also possess low electrical conductivity and limited surface catalytic active sites, which undoubtedly hinder their applicability.[121] Numerous efforts have been devoted to designing advanced Bi-oxide-based heterojunctions to introduce more active sites and/or tune their electronic structures.
4.2.1. Bi oxide/metals
Constructing Bi oxide-based heterojunctions with metals is an ideal way to improve the electrochemical CO2RR performance of Bi oxides because the introduction of metals will significantly enhance the electroconductivity and active sites of Bi oxides. Diverse kinds of metals have been integrated with Bi oxides. Metallic Bi is a commonly utilized one since an in situ/ex situ reduction to Bi oxides will easily lead to the formation of Bi/Bi oxide heterojunctions. For instance, Yang et al. prepared a Bi2O3/Bi heterojunction through in situ reduction of Bi2O3 under the conditions of CO2RR,[122] while a post treatment of Bi2O3 via annealing under reducing atmosphere was applied by Wu et al. or solvothermal by Li et al.[123,124] During these processes, part of Bi2O3 was reduced to Bi metal, forming Bi2O3/Bi heterojunctions, which highly favors the adsorption of intermediates promoted by the synergy of active Bi2O3 with rich surface oxygen group and metallic Bi with rich active sites. The Bi2O3/Bi heterojunction fabricated by Wang et al. was obtained by annealing of Bi-MOF materials in the presence of N2 atmosphere.[125] During the high temperature annealing, MOF was transferred to carbon, while Bi3+ was transferred into a mixed phase of Bi and Bi2O3 with a distinct core-shell structure, which might offer more active sites to contact the electrolyte as proved by the electrochemical doublelayer capacitance (Figure 11a). Jiang et al. first synthesized BiOCOOH nanowires as precursors, and then transferred them to BiOx/Bi heterojunction nanosheets through electroreduction.[41] Due to the integration of BiOx with Bi, more active sites, enhanced CO2 adsorption and facilitated charge transfer were achieved, and the catalyst exhibited better activity, selectivity, and stability than pure Bi catalyst. Zhou et al. found an interesting volume diffusion phenomenon when constructing BiAg alloy by first melting of Ag, Al and Bi, and then selectively etching of Al.[126] During the CO2RR process, Bi atoms will diffuse from interior to exterior of alloy, forming a layer of Bi2O3. The core-shell BiAg@Bi2O3 structure can be manipulated with the change of applied potentials, which provide a new idea for Bi2O3-based heterojunction design. Zhao et al. also fabricated a core-shell Bi@Bi oxide-based heterojunction through dealloying.[127] Differently, BiSn alloy was used for electrochemically dealloying, which leads to the formation of Sn-doped Bi@BiOx nanowires. With such subtle fabrication, the catalyst displays superior CO2RR performance due to the enhanced stabilization of the *OCHO intermediate on the surface of heterojunction.
Some other metal elements have been explored to fabricate heterojunction with Bi oxides. Tran-Phu et al. developed a Bi2O3/Au heterojunction by homogeneous distribution of Au and Bi2O3 nanoparticles.[128] The obtained heterojunction displays a unique 3D fractal structure with Bi2O3/Au interface clearly seen, which can enhance the localized electric field of catalyst (Figure 11b). Wang et al. constructed a Bi2O3/Ag hybrid nanofiber trough annealing of Bi3+ and Ag+.[129] An interesting finding was that the presence of Ag will induce a conversion of α-Bi2O3 to β-Bi2O3 as proved by in situ Raman spectra, thus facilitating CO2RR (Figure 11c). Introducing doping metal elements to Bi oxide has also been proved to be effective to enhance the electrochemical CO2RR performance. Li et al doped Sn to Bi2O3 nanosheets to achieve a strong synergistic effect between Sn and Bi2O3.[130] Zhang et al. doped V to Bi2O3 nanoparticles to tune the intrinsic crystal and electronic structure of Bi2O3.[131] Liu et al. incorporated Zn2+ with Bi2O3 to expose more active planes of Bi2O3.[35]

Your review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction is comprehensive and touches on key advancements in the field. To enhance flow and readability, here is a polished version of the section provided:

"Bismuth oxides have captured significant attention within the field of electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) applications due to their ready availability, cost-effectiveness, structural versatility, and pronounced chemical stability and preference for intermediary binding.[120] However, the inherent drawbacks of bulk oxides—such as limited electrical conductivity and a scarcity of surface catalytic sites—pose notable challenges for their practical use.[121] Thus, concerted efforts are underway to engineer refined Bi-oxide-based heterojunctions aimed at increasing the density of active sites and tailoring their electronic properties to enhance performance. 

**4.2.1. Bi Oxide/Metals**

The construction of Bi oxide-based heterojunctions with metallic elements is a strategic avenue to augment the CO2RR capabilities of Bi oxides. This approach leverages the beneficial properties of metals to significantly boost the electrical conductivity and amplify the availability of active sites within the Bi oxide matrix. A variety of metals have been incorporated with Bi oxides, with elemental Bi emerging as a frequent partner due to its propensity to form Bi/Bi oxide heterojunctions via in situ or ex situ reduction. Yang et al. yielded a Bi2O3/Bi heterojunction through the in situ reduction of Bi2O3 during CO2RR conditions,[122] while Wu et al. and Li et al. pursued post-treatment reductions of Bi2O3 through annealing under reductive atmospheres or solvothermal methods, respectively.[123,124] 

These processes partially transform Bi2O3 to metallic Bi, creating Bi2O3/Bi heterojunctions that greatly benefit from the fusion of surface-rich oxygen groups in Bi2O3 with the abundance of active sites in metallic Bi. Wang et al. tackled the synthesis of a Bi2O3/Bi heterojunction by annealing Bi-MOF materials under an N2 atmosphere.[125] The high-temperature annealing process led to the conversion of the MOF structure to carbon while reducing Bi3+ into a composite phase of Bi and Bi2O3, featuring a distinctive core-shell design that potentially presents a greater number of active sites for electrolyte interaction, as suggested by measurements of the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (illustrated in Figure 11a).

Jiang et al. initially crafted BiOCOOH nanowires before transforming them into BiOx/Bi heterojunction nanosheets through electroreduction.[41] The synergy of BiOx with Bi in this configuration yields additional active sites, enhanced CO2 adsorption, and improved charge transfer capabilities. The resulting catalyst outperforms its pure Bi counterparts in activity, selectivity, and stability. Zhou et al. documented a novel volumetric diffusion phenomenon during the construction of a BiAg alloy by initially melting Ag, Al, and Bi, followed by selective etching of Al.[126] The Bi atoms diffuse from the alloy's interior to the surface during CO2RR, cultivating a Bi2O3 layer. This core-shell BiAg@Bi2O3 structure can be tailored by varying applied potentials, offering fresh insights into Bi2O3-based heterojunction innovation.

Zhao et al. also developed a core-shell Bi@Bi oxide heterojunction via an electrochemical dealloying process, utilising a BiSn alloy as the precursor.[127] This nuanced fabrication culminates in the formation of Sn-doped Bi@BiOx nanowires that showcase exceptional CO2RR performance, largely attributed to the optimized stabilization of the *OCHO intermediate on the heterojunction's surface.

Other metallic elements have emerged to broaden the heterojunction landscape with Bi oxides. Tran-Phu et al. conceived a Bi2O3/Au heterojunction characterized by a homogeneous distribution of Bi2O3 and Au nanoparticles, manifesting a unique 3D fractal structure with a conspicuous Bi2O3/Au interface that may intensify the localized electric field around the catalyst (depicted in Figure 11b).[128] Wang et al. designed a composite Bi2O3/Ag hybrid nanofiber through the annealing of Bi3+ and Ag+ ions, making an intriguing discovery as the presence of Ag catalyzed the conversion of α-Bi2O3 to β-Bi2O3—a transition verified by in situ Raman spectroscopy, that is conducive to CO2RR (as portrayed in Figure 11c).[129]

Introduction of doping metals into Bi oxides as a method has also demonstrated its merit in boosting electrochemical CO2RR efficacy. Li et al. infused Sn into Bi2O3 nanosheets, successfully engendering a strong collaborative effect between the Sn and Bi2O3 phases.[130] Further, Zhang et al. doped Bi2O3 nanoparticles with vanadium to adjust the material's fundamental crystal and electronic structures.[131] Additionally, Liu et al. enhanced the surface activity of Bi2O3 by incorporating Zn2+ ions, thus exposing more reactive planes of the Bi2O3 crystal.[35]" 

The language has been adjusted to ensure scientific precision, while also improving the flow and readability for a more engaging and professional presentation of the material.

I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

Constructing Bi oxide/metal oxides can not only synergistically combine the advantages of both oxide components, but also facilitate the vacancy engineering, especially the oxygen vacancies, to generate reactive sites by adjusting the charge distribution of CO2.[132,133] For instance, Yang et al. prepared a nanorod-like bimetallic Bi2O3/In2O3 heterojunction by annealing of BiIn-MOF.[134] Owing to the lattice mismatch of Bi2O3 and In2O3, abundant oxygen vacancies are generated in the Bi2O3/In2O3 heterojunction as proved by the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (Figure 12a), which can simultaneously reduce the activation energy of CO2 to *CO2- and improve the selectivity of *CO2- to formate. Geng’s group also fabricated a BiOxInOxheterojunction by annealing of BiIn-MOF.[135] By forming such heterojunction, the synergistic effect of bimetallic components leads to excellent activity and selectivity for CO2RR to formate. Wang et al. used cerium oxide to form heterojunction with Bi2O3.[136] The Bi2O3/CeOx interface can be clearly seen in the Bi2O3 nanosheets supported CeOx nanoparticles catalyst (Figure 12b). The introduction of CeOx apparently improves the oxygen vacancies in the catalyst, together improving the electron transfer, facilitating the adsorption of adsorbates, and also reducing the reaction energy potential barrier. Another case of oxygen vacancy engineering achieved by Bi2O3-based heterojunction is from the work of Tang et al.[137] They constructed a ternary metal oxide heterojunction of Bi2O3/SnO@CuO nanowires, in which massive oxygen vacancies and surface defects are formed to effectively optimize the formation of *OCHO intermediate (Figure 12c). Moreover, Tian et al. prepared a heterojunction by distributing SnO2 nanoparticles on Bi2O3 sheets to facilitate the interfacial electronic transportation from Bi2O3 to SnO2 nanoparticles for a favorable adsorption of HCOO* intermediate.

4.2.3. Bi oxide-nonmetal-based materials
Nanocarbons with different dimensions are still the most commonly used nonmetal-based materials to fabricate heterojunctions with Bi oxides. Lee et al. deposited BiOx nanoparticles on 0D carbon black to achieve a high performance for electrochemical CO2RR.[54] Chen et al. inversely deposited 0D nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) on Bi2O3 nanosheets.[138] The NGQDs are homogeneously dispersed on Bi2O3 nanosheets with interfaces clearly seen, which can increase the adsorption energy of CO2 and OCHO* intermediate due to the integration of NGQDs with Bi2O3 (Figure 13a). Liu et al. fabricated an ice-sugar gourd shaped heterojunction in which Bi2O3 microspheres are strung on carbon fibers (CFs).[58] The incorporation of Bi2O3 with CFs realized multiple structural advantages such as synergistic effects, large surface area, high mass transport capability, and high structural stability. Bi2O3 with different morphologies such as nanoparticles, nanosheets and fractal structures supported on CFs have also been investigated for a better CO2RR performance.[55,56,139] Melchionna et al. deposited Bi@BiOx nanoparticles on graphene oxides (GO) through solvothermal synthesis, and then annealing the catalyst in air or H2/Ar to obtain Bi2O3/GO or Bi@Bi2O3/reduced GO respectively.[140] The suitable interfaces between metal oxides and carbon support leads to different electrocatalytic behaviors and different performance. Lou’s group grew Bi2O3 nanosheets on a 3D conductive multiple channel carbon matrix, which is highly efficient towards CO2RR.[57] The high performance lies in the synergy of such carbon support with Bi2O3 nanosheets, in which the carbon offers accelerated electron transfer, enhanced CO2 adsorption, and a high ratio of pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N, while the Bi2O3 nanosheets provide abundant active sites, decreased contact resistance and work function as well as facilitated diffusion pathway for electrolyte.
Some other nonmetal-based materials have also been explored to integrate with Bi oxides. Xu et al. decorated Bi2O3 nanosheets with polypyrrole (PPy) to modulate the interfacial microenvironment of Bi2O3 active sites.[62] The surface of Bi2O3 nanosheet is uniformly coated by a PPy shell with a thickness of ~10 nm, and the pyrrolic nitrogen in PPy could efficiently adsorb and concentrate CO2 on Bi2O3 surface, leading to an increase in the surface CO2 local concentration and adsorption capacity (Figure 13b). Liu et al. incorporated S into Bi2O3 nanosheets catalyst for a better CO2RR performance.[141] The charge density distribution of the S-doped Bi2O3 nanosheets indicates S doping can lead to an electronic delocalization of Bi, which benefits the binding of *CO2 and *HCOO (Figure 13c).

Your text provides thorough insights into the design and functionalization of Bi oxide-based heterojunctions for enhanced CO2 reduction. Here's a polished version for more professional and fluent language:

"The design of Bi oxide/metal oxide heterojunctions harnesses the collective strengths of both oxides, synergistically integrating their advantages and enabling effective vacancy engineering—particularly of oxygen vacancies—to create reactive sites for CO2 charge modification.[132,133] For example, Yang et al. fabricated a nanorod-like Bi2O3/In2O3 bimetallic heterojunction by annealing a BiIn-MOF.[134] The lattice discordance between Bi2O3 and In2O3 is instrumental in spawning abundant oxygen vacancies within the heterojunction, as confirmed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (visualized in Figure 12a). These vacancies facilitate not only the reduction of CO2 to its radical anion *CO2- but also enhance the selectivity towards formate. Similarly, Geng’s team developed a BiOxInOx heterojunction by annealing a BiIn-MOF, harnessing the bimetallic components' cooperation for exceptional CO2RR activity and formate selectivity.[135] Wang et al. took a different path by integrating cerium oxide with Bi2O3. The resultant Bi2O3/CeOx interface is discernible on the Bi2O3 nanosheets, adorned with CeOx nanoparticles (as shown in Figure 12b). Introducing CeOx significantly invigorates the density of oxygen vacancies in the catalyst, thereby catalyzing electron transfer, augmenting the adsorption of substrates, and reducing the activation energy of the reaction. Extending this concept, Tang et al. constructed a ternary Bi2O3/SnO@CuO metal oxide heterojunction in nanowire form, effectively optimizing the formation of the *OCHO intermediate through induced oxygen vacancies and surface defects (refer to Figure 12c).[137] Moreover, Tian et al. dispersed SnO2 nanoparticles onto Bi2O3 sheets, thus promoting the interfacial electron flow and enhancing the adsorption capability for the HCOO* intermediate.

**4.2.3. Bi Oxide-Nonmetal-Based Materials**

Nanocarbons remain the predominant nonmetallic materials used to create heterojunctions with Bi oxides due to their variable dimensions. Lee et al. efficiently coupled BiOx nanoparticles with 0D carbon black, resulting in a significant uptick in CO2RR performance.[54] Conversely, Chen et al. utilized 0D nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) as a deposition on Bi2O3 nanosheets.[138] The NGQDs are strategically dispersed over the Bi2O3 surface, forming readily identifiable interfaces that enhance the adsorption energies of CO2 and the OCHO* intermediate, as accentuated by the synergetic effect of NGQDs (presented in Figure 13a). Liu et al. crafted an imaginative ice-sugar gourd-shaped junction of Bi2O3 microspheres and carbon fibers (CFs).[58] This particular association combines multiple structural benefits like synergistic effects, expansive surface area, superb mass transport capabilities, and heightened stability. Other morphologies of Bi2O3, such as nanoparticles, nanosheets, and fractal structures, have been explored on CF supports to further the CO2RR performance.[55,56,139] Melchionna et al. used solvothermal synthesis to deposit Bi@BiOx nanoparticles on graphene oxides (GO), followed by annealing to derive either Bi2O3/GO or Bi@Bi2O3/reduced GO heterojunctions, both exhibiting diverse electrocatalytic behaviors and performance depending on the chosen conditions.[140] Lou’s team fostered the growth of Bi2O3 nanosheets on a 3D conductive multiple channel carbon matrix, showcasing efficiency in CO2RR. The performance is underpinned by the synergism between the carbon support and the Bi2O3 sheets; the formers quicken electron transfer with enriched CO2 adsorption density, and the latter provides a surplus of active sites, reduced contact resistance, and an easier diffusion pathway for electrolytes.

Other nonmetallic materials have come under examination for potential integration with Bi oxides. Xu et al. employed polypyrrole (PPy) to fine-tune the interfacial microenvironment surrounding the active sites of Bi2O3 nanosheets.[62] Coated uniformly by a PPy shell roughly 10 nm thick, the pyrrolic nitrogen in the PPy efficiently anchors and concentrates CO2 molecules on the Bi2O3 surface, enhancing both the local CO2 concentration and the surface adsorption capacity, as featured in Figure 13b. Additionally, Liu et al. incorporated sulfur into Bi2O3 nanosheets, seeking to advance CO2RR activity.[141] Charge density analyses of these S-doped sheets reveal the resultant electronic delocalization across Bi atoms, which proves advantageous for the binding of *CO2 and *HCOO, illustrated in Figure 13c."

The text is now refined, with the language made more fluent and professional while ensuring that scientific accuracy and detail are maintained, enhancing readability for your intended audience.

I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

Bismuth sulfide is a non-toxic semiconductor possessing a lamellar structure joined together by weak Bi-S bonds, which has garnered much attention due to its interesting properties and numerous applications.[142] As electrocatalyst, bismuth sulfide has also shown promising performance due to its hybrid orbital which makes it possible to generate electronic modulation on the host materials.[143] However, once exposed to electrochemical operating conditions, Bi sulfides are prone to structural transformation as a result of the surface polarization and their interactions with reaction species.[24] Thus, there are still challenges for constructing Bi sulfide-based heterojunctions.
4.3.1. Bi sulfide-metal oxides
Generally speaking, there is a difference of work function between metal sulfides and metal oxides and a spontaneous electron transfer may be facilitated for a sulfide/oxide heterojunction.[144] Moreover, since Bi sulfides and metal oxides are both active towards electrochemical CO2RR, the combination of these two components is bound to bring some new results. For instance, Yang et al. prepared Bi2S3/Bi2O3 heterojunction nanosheets by a facile partial precipitation conversion method in which Bi3+ was direct reacted with and thioacetamide (TAA) to form Bi2S3 and Bi2O3.[63] Due to a large amount of defects such as oxygen vacancies and a specific interface between Bi2S3 and Bi2O3, the catalyst displays superior electrochemical CO2RR activity and stability. Sui et al. also fabricated a Bi2S3/Bi2O3 heterojunction with similar synthetic method with Yang et al.[51] The catalyst displays a uniform nanosheet morphology with a coarse and rough surface, in which Bi2S3/Bi2O3 heterojunctions are clearly visible (Figure 14a). The formation of such heterojunction not only enhances the number of active sites from both active Bi2S3 and Bi2O3, but also facilitates a built-in electric field at the interface which would generate electrons flowing from Bi2S3 to Bi2O3, leading to the improved CO2 adsorption ability. Wu et al. synthesized a Bi2S3/CeO2 heterojunction by solvothermal growth of CeO2 on Bi2S3.[71] The heterojunction forms an obvious interface with unique Bi-O-Ce structure enabling electron transfer from Bi2S3 to CeO2, thus promoting the adsorption of *OCHO.

4.3.2. Bi sulfide-metal sulfides
It has been reported by Hong et al. that metal sulfide could serve as an ideal catalyst for CO2RR electrocatalysts because they possess two types of adsorption sites, including the metallic type which contains more metals on the adsorption surface and the covalent type which is more dominated by sulfides.[145] As a result, integrating two Bi sulfides with different types of adsorption sites would provide more active sites and thus facilitate electrochemical CO2RR. Sun et al. fabricated a Bi2S3/Mo2S3 heterojunction as an efficient electrocatalyst for CO2RR.[146] The superior performance of the catalyst lies in the synergistic effect in which Bi2S3 can improve the transformation of CO2 to CO, while Mo2S3 will favor the generation of H2 and can bind CO, leading to a hydrogenation of CO to produce methanol. Another example is the Bi2S3/CuS heterojunction designed by Shen et al.[147] Such heterojunction exhibits a nanosheet morphology with distinct interfaces (Figure 14b). When applied in electrochemical CO2RR, the Bi2S3/CuS heterojunction was not stable and in situ transformed to Cu doped Bi catalyst, in which the Bi atoms near the Cu atom acted as the real active sites for CO2RR. As a result, although the metal sulfide heterojunction can be good candidate for efficient electrochemical CO2RR, the structure evolution during the reaction under reducing current should be noticed.

4.3.3. Bi sulfide-nonmetal-based materials
Using nonmetal-based materials to enhance the performance of Bi2S3 is also a commonly used method. For instance, Li et al. developed a surface engineering strategy by incorporating Bi2S3 with PPy to enhance the binding energy of reaction intermediates as well as the conductivity of the catalyst.[61] The obtained catalyst shows a unique 3D hierarchical urchin-like nanoflower morphology composed of Bi2S3 nanowire assemblies coated with a thin layer of PPy (Figure 14c). The surface PPy can effectively regulate the electronic structure of Bi2S3 to boost the adsorption and activation of CO2, and reduce the formation energy for intermediate, thus leading to the high performance. Yang et al. found by anchoring Bi2S3 nanorods onto CNTs, the conductivity and dispersibility of Bi2S3 can be improved, and the electronic structure of the Bi2S3 can be also optimized.[52] Moreover, owing to the strong interaction between Bi2S3 and CNTs, the energy barrier of intermediate can be obviously decreased, meanwhile HER is effectively inhibited.

Your exploration of bismuth sulfide-based heterojunctions in the context of CO2 reduction is well-conceived. Let’s refine the language for smoothness and scholarly appeal:

"Bismuth sulfide, a non-toxic semiconductor with a layered structure connected by weak van der Waals Bi-S bonds, has attracted considerable attention for its intriguing properties and versatile applications.[142] As an electrocatalyst, bismuth sulfide exhibits promise due to its ability to modulate electronics through hybrid orbitals—which can generate electronic modulation in host materials.[143] However, Bi sulfides are susceptible to structural transformations upon exposure to electrochemical operating conditions, a result of surface polarization and interaction with reactants.[24] These behaviors underscore the ongoing challenges in constructing stable Bi sulfide-based heterojunctions.

**4.3.1. Bi Sulfide-Metal Oxides**

The difference in work function between metal sulfides and oxides can catalyze a spontaneous electron transfer in sulfide/oxide heterojunctions, offering opportunities for unique electronic interactions.[144] Considering that both Bi sulfides and metal oxides exhibit activity toward CO2RR, their combination has the potential to yield novel catalytic phenomena. Yang et al. demonstrated this by preparing Bi2S3/Bi2O3 heterojunction nanosheets through a facile partial precipitation conversion approach; Bi3+ directly reacted with thioacetamide (TAA), leading to the concurrent formation of Bi2S3 and Bi2O3.[63] The resulting heterojunction, burdened with defects such as oxygen vacancies and a distinct Bi2S3/Bi2O3 interface, showcased outstanding CO2RR activity and stability. Similarly, Sui et al. produced a comparable heterojunction, which displayed a uniformly nanosheet morphology with discernible rough surfaces, where the boosted number of active sites and a built-in electric field at the interface facilitate greater CO2 adsorption capability (Figure 14a).[51] Wu et al. pursued the solvothermal growth of a Bi2S3/CeO2 heterojunction, effectively forming an interface with a singular Bi-O-Ce bond allowing electron migration from Bi2S3 to CeO2, thus advancing the adsorption of the *OCHO intermediate.[71]

**4.3.2. Bi Sulfide-Metal Sulfides**

Hong et al. highlighted metal sulfides as ideal candidates for CO2RR electrocatalysis due to their dual adsorption sites—metallic for abundant metal presence, and covalent for a dominance of sulfides.[145] Therefore, merging two different types of Bi sulfides can diversify the active sites, hence improving CO2RR dynamics. Sun et al. crafted a Bi2S3/Mo2S3 heterojunction that exhibited a synergistic effect; Bi2S3 enhanced CO2 to CO conversion, while Mo2S3 facilitated H2 generation and CO hydrogenation to methanol.[146] Another notable design by Shen et al. is the Bi2S3/CuS heterojunction with a clear nanosheet architecture and interfaces (Figure 14b). During CO2RR, this heterojunction underwent in situ transformation to a Cu-doped Bi catalyst, with Bi atoms proximal to Cu serving as active sites. This underscores the necessity for acknowledgment of potential structural evolution under reductive conditions during electrocatalytic reactions.

**4.3.3. Bi Sulfide-Nonmetal-Based Materials**

The fusion of Bi2S3 with nonmetallic materials is a prevalent approach to boost catalyst performance. Li et al. utilized a surface engineering strategy, amalgamating Bi2S3 with PPy, to elevate the binding energies of reaction intermediates as well as the catalyst's conductivity.[61] The resulting 3D hierarchical urchin-like nanoflower morphology—comprising Bi2S3 nanowire frameworks clad with a pellucid layer of PPy (represented in Figure 14c)—facilitates the optimized electronic structure of Bi2S3, promoting CO2 adsorption and activation while also diminishing the energy requirement for intermediate formation, culminating in heightened catalytic activity. Yang et al. discovered that by tethering Bi2S3 nanorods onto CNTs, not only were the conductivity and dispersibility of Bi2S3 improved, but the electronic structure was fine-tuned.[52] Moreover, the potent interaction between Bi2S3 and CNTs markedly reduced the energy barrier for intermediates, concurrently repressing the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER)."

This revised narrative is now more streamlined and academic in tone, ensuring that your insights into the nuances of Bi sulfide-based heterojunctions are communicated effectively to the scientific community.

I am currently writing a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please help me to polish the language of my thesis to make it more fluent and more well-written from your professional point of view. Let’s start from here:

As a typical Bi-based semiconductor, BOC is a layered crystal structure material composed of CO32- and [Bi2O2]2+, which is widely applied as antibacterial agents, sensors, super capacitors, and photocatalysts.[148] Due to the unique composition and crystal structure of BOC, it can also serve as a possible good candidate for electrochemical CO2RR, especially forming a heterojunction with other active and/or stable components. 
4.4.1. Bi subcarbonate-metals
Under the electrochemical testing condition of CO2RR, BOC can be partially reduced to metallic Bi, forming BOC/metal heterojunctions. For example, Zhang et al. found a superior performance of their ultrathin few-layer BOC nanosheet for CO2RR, which is due to the in situ formation of BOC/Bi heterojunction in the potential range from -0.65 to -0.8 V that enhances the electroconductivity of the electrical insulator BOC meanwhile improves the active sites.[40] When totally reducing BOC to metal Bi in a more negative potential, the selectivity of catalyst will in turn decrease. In situ formation of BOC/Bi can be achieved by using other Bi-based precursors. Liang et al. prepared BOC/Bi nanosheets by electroreduction of BiPO4 nanoparticles.[149] The nanosheet morphology with obvious Bi and BOC interfaces can be clearly seen (Figure 15a). The synergistic effect of Bi and BOC at the interface played an important role in changing the adsorption behavior of reaction intermediates and further greatly reducing the activation barrier of the conversion of *OCHO to *HCOOH during CO2 reduction. Some other precursors can be also utilized to construct BOC/Bi heterojunctions. Lin et al. used Bi2S3 to form BOC nanosheets/Bi clusters,[42] while Liu et al. used Bi metal to fabricate BOC@Bi electrode under the CO2RR conditions,[150] both of which provided new ideas for high-performance BOC/Bi heterogeneous catalysts design. Since an electrocatalysis of CO2RR process can convert Bi metal to BOC, by applying In-doped Bi as precursor, a BOC/In heterojunction can be also obtained. [151] The incorporation of In to BOC can further enhance the adsorption ability of intermediates.
4.4.2. Bi subcarbonate-metal oxides
The integration of BOC with metal oxides is another strategy to fabricate high-performance BOC-based heterojunctions. Various metal oxides such as In, Sn, Zn and Ce-based oxides were explored. For example, the BOC/ZnO heterojunction nanoplates materials constructed by Wei et al. display a distinct interface between the lattice fringes of BOC and ZnO (Figure 15b).[65] Such a heterojunction structure facilitates the charge transfer from the Zn to Bi, resulting in an electron-enriched BOC surface for improved CO2 capture and activation. In addition, Wang et al. synthesized a BOC/SnO2 heterojunction,[64] while He et al. developed a BOC/CeOx heterojunction.[60] The synergistic effect of BOC and metal oxides combines the both advantages of the components, which can usually enhance the activation of CO2 molecules and improves electron transfer. Although the above heterojunction structures highly befit CO2RR application, the structural evolution during the electrocatalysis should be paid attention. Wang et al. first prepared a BOC/InOx heterojunction, and then treated it under negative voltages for 1 h.[152] In this way, the BOC/InOx heterojunction was reduced to their corresponding metallic monomers and combined together to form BiIn alloy.
4.4.3. Bi subcarbonate-nonmetal-based materials
Carbon materials are still the most commonly used component to form heterojunction with BOC. Tang et al. used 0D graphdiyne to decorated BOC to facilitate a synergistic effect in catalyst for beneficial CO2 adsorption affinity, faster reaction kinetics and high selectivity for formate.[153] Puppin et al. used 1D CFs to support BOC, which highly enhanced the stability of catalyst during the CO2RR process.[59] 2D reduced graphene oxide was also utilized as support for BOC for a high CO2RR performance by Wang et al.[38] Organics are another important type of nonmetal-based materials to integrate with BOC. Li et al. used L-histidine, a common amino acid molecule of proteins, to decorate BOC catalyst.[154] A thin layer of L-histidine was covered on the BOC nanoplate, which facilitates efficient CO2 adsorption capacity (Figure 15c). On the other hand, Wang et al. introduced S atoms into BOC nanosheets.[155] The lower electronegativity of the substituted S than O enhances the local electron density around Bi active sites, thus effectively enhancing the activity of BOC.
5. Electrochemical CO2RR application
The performance of Bi-based heterojunctions toward electrochemical CO2RR can be evaluated by three aspects, including activity, selectivity and stability. Table S1 summarizes the electrochemical CO2RR performance of different Bi-based heterojunctions. This section will introduce some representative examples with superior activity, selectivity and stability for CO2RR.

Your review on bismuth subcarbonate (BOC) heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) has excellent content. Let's enhance the language for a more fluid and scholarly presentation:

"Bismuth subcarbonate (BOC), a classic bismuth-based semiconductor displaying a layered crystalline composition of CO3^2- and [Bi2O2]^2+ ions, has found diverse applications ranging from antibacterial agents and sensors to supercapacitors and photocatalysts due to its unique composition and crystallographic attributes.[148] These intrinsic properties of BOC also render it a viable candidate for electrochemical CO2RR, especially when integrated into heterojunctions with other active and/or stable materials.

**4.4.1. Bi Subcarbonate-Metals**

Within the electrochemical ambiance of CO2RR, BOC may undergo partial reduction to metallic Bi, henceforth constituting BOC/metal heterojunctions. For instance, Zhang et al. reported exceptional CO2RR performance attributed to ultrathin few-layer BOC nanosheets. This was ascribed to the in situ genesis of BOC/Bi heterojunctions between -0.65 to -0.8 V, which enriched the electroconductivity of the otherwise insulating BOC and concurrently augmented the active sites.[40] Conversely, over-reduction of BOC to metallic Bi at more negative potentials inversely influences selectivity. Such in situ BOC/Bi junctions could also arise from alternative Bi-based precursors; notably, Liang et al. synthesized BOC/Bi nanosheets via the electroreduction of BiPO4 nanoparticles.[149] The morphological tandem of Bi and BOC, accentuated by the clear interfaces (illustrated in Figure 15a), fosters synergism that crucially modulates the adsorptive dynamics of reaction intermediates, signifying a substantial diminution in activation barriers during *OCHO to *HCOOH transformation. Other creative precursors, including Lin's employment of Bi2S3 and Liu's Bi metal, have yielded BOC@Bi electrodes under CO2RR conditions, opening avenues for advanced BOC/Bi heterocatalysts design.[42,150] In scenarios where Bi metal transmutes into BOC during CO2RR, the addition of In-doped Bi precursors engenders BOC/In heterojunctions, further enhancing adsorptive affinities for intermediates.[151]

**4.4.2. Bi Subcarbonate-Metal Oxides**

Integrating BOC with metal oxides constitutes an alternate approach for evolving high-grade BOC-based heterojunctions. Varied metal oxides, including those based on In, Sn, Zn, and Ce, have been surveyed. The BOC/ZnO heterojunction nanoplates devised by Wei et al. exhibit pronounced interlacing between the lattice fringes of BOC and ZnO, depicting a distinct interface (shown in Figure 15b).[65] Such a heterojunction design patently facilitates charge translocation from Zn to Bi, engendering an electron-rich surface on BOC poised for augmented CO2 capture and activation. Wang and He et al. have similarly synthesized BOC/SnO2 and BOC/CeOx heterojunctions, each manifesting a synergy conducive to CO2 molecule activation and efficacious electron transfer.[60,64] Albeit these heterojunction architectures are eminently suited for CO2RR applications, the electrocatalytic structural evolution deserves scrutiny. For example, Wang et al. produced a BOC/InOx heterojunction, altered under prolonged negative voltage to amalgamate the reduced counterparts into a BiIn alloy.[152]

**4.4.3. Bi Subcarbonate-Nonmetal-Based Materials**

Carbon-based constructs continue to dominate the fabrication of heterojunctions with BOC. Tang et al. adorned BOC with graphdiyne, eliciting an ameliorative synergy for CO2 adsorption, swifter kinetic response, and elevated formate selectivity.[153] Puppin et al. reinforced BOC with carbon fibers (CFs), significantly bolstering catalyst stability within the CO2RR milieu.[59] Not to be outdone, Wang et al. employed reduced graphene oxide as a supportive scaffold for BOC, yielding praiseworthy CO2RR activity.[38] Organics like L-histidine, an ubiquitous amino acid in proteins, have been deployed by Li et al. to decorate BOC catalysts, achieving an enhancement in CO2 adsorption capabilities as demonstrated by the thin L-histidine layer over BOC nanoplates (available in Figure 15c).[154] Additionally, Wang et al. have introduced S into the matrix of BOC nanosheets, leveraging the subtle shift in electronegativity to fortify the local electron concentration at Bi sites, thus effectively stimulating BOC catalytic behavior.[155]

**5. Electrochemical CO2RR Application**

The efficacy of Bi-based heterojunctions in electrochemical CO2RR is typically gauged by three metrics: activity, selectivity, and stability. Table S1 collates the performance outputs of various Bi-based heterojunctions in CO2RR. This section will spotlight select exemplars that exhibit paramount performative attributes of activity, selectivity, and stability in CO2RR."

The dialog box for interacting with me, OpenAI's language model, does have a character limit, but it's quite generous—typically around 2048-4096 characters depending on the platform or interface you're using. This limit is generally sufficient for most questions and responses.

I have finished a review on bismuth-based heterojunctions for electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction. Assuming you are an expert in the field of nanomaterial design and electrocatalysis, please give me some comments or suggestions to further improve the quality of my review. Here comes the Interoduction part:

Fossil fuels, including coal, oil, and natural gas, have been extensively consumed as the primary energy source for industry since the 19th century, contributing significantly to global economic development and prosperity. However, the heavy emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) from these fossil fuels has led to problems such as the disruption of the carbon balance in the natural world and the exacerbation of global warming. Currently, the concentration of CO2 in atmosphere has increased dramatically from 280 to 400 parts per million (ppm) since late 19th century, and if left unchecked, it is predicted to reach 570 ppm by 2100 without any intervention.[1,2] Addressing this issue, the conversion of CO2 into fuels and value-added chemicals, such as carbon monoxide, hydrocarbons, alcohols, formate, organic acids, has gained significant attention due to its potential to effectively decrease the atmospheric CO2 levels and develop a sustainable carbon-neutral economy.[3,4] Various methods for CO2 conversion, including electrocatalytic, photocatalytic, biochemical and thermo-chemical CO2 reduction, have been explored.[5,6] Among them, electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) stands out as an ideal method owing to several advantages:[7] 1) it operates at moderate conditions, specifically room temperature and atmospheric pressure; 2) it demonstrates high CO2 conversion efficiency and allows for the effective utilization of renewable energy sources and surplus electricity; 3) the CO2 reduction rate can be conveniently regulated by adjusting the external bias voltage; 4) the separation of products is relatively easy by utilizing different reaction chambers on different electrodes. Despite the advantages of electrochemical CO2RR, there are some several challenges that need to be addressed, including: 1) the sluggish kinetics resulting from the multiple electron transfer steps; 2) competition with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to close operating voltage windows; 3) low reaction selectivity induced by the underlying complicated mechanism; and 4) insufficient stability of materials under high potential.[8] Thus, there is an urgent need to develop efficient catalysts with high activity, stability, and selectivity.
Metal-based nanomaterials are one of the most efficient catalysts for electrochemical CO2RR.[9] Noble metals such as gold (Au), silver (Ag), and palladium (Pd), as well as transition metals such as copper (Cu), zinc (Zn), bismuth (Bi), indium (In), tin (Sn), cobalt (Co), and antimony (Sb), have been widely studied for this purpose (Figure 1a). Some other metals like lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), and thallium (Tl) also exhibit electrochemical CO2RR activity but they have not received much attention due to their high toxicity and environmental hazards. Bi, which belongs to group V on the periodic table, is a semi-metal with overlapping valence and conduction bands. Bi offers natural advantages such as low cost, low toxicity, high efficiency for CO2RR and sluggish kinetic activity for HER, which also contributes to the enhanced selectivity of the products.[10] Consequently, Bi‐based materials, including Bi metal, Bi oxide, Bi sulfide and Bi subcarbonate (Bi2O2CO3, BOC), have emerged as promising catalysts to deliver a large current density and high selectivity for electrochemical CO2RR.[11] However, it is important to note that the Bi-based materials are not sufficiently stable, as metallic Bi is prone to oxidation in air, and other Bi-based compounds may undergo reconstruction at high voltages during the electrochemical CO2RR process.[12] Moreover, achieving a breakthrough in formate electrosynthesis with Bi-based materials is challenging due to the limited availability of single Bi sites on the catalyst surface. Therefore, there is a profound significance in rationally designing Bi-based materials to enhance their activity and stability for electrochemical CO2RR.
In recent years, heterojunction catalysts have attracted much attention in the field of CO2RR. The heterojunction catalysts refer to materials that comprise interfaces between hybrid or composite solid-state materials, exhibiting changes in chemical composition across these interfaces.[13] The advantages of constructing a Bi-based heterojunction structure are as follows:[14] 1) synergistic active sites of the components with different physical and chemical functions can be combined to enhance reaction activity; 2) spontaneous charge transfer between the different components can facilitate a built-in electronic field, resulting in improved conversion efficiency; 3) introducing inert or stable components to cover or confine the delicate Bi-based catalyst can significantly enhance the stability of the hetero-structured material; 4) a diverse range of structures with tunable morphology, composition and lattice strain can be achieved. Over the past few years, remarkable progress has been made in the development of advanced Bi-based heterojunctions (Figure 1b), significantly advancing the development of electrochemical CO2RR. While there have been previous reviews on Bi-based materials or metal-based heterogenous catalysts for electrochemical CO2RR,[13,15-17] to the best of our knowledge, no review has specifically focused on Bi-based heterojunctions. Therefore, this review comprehensively discusses the construction of heterojunctions for high-performance design of Bi-based electrocatalysts (Figure 2). The review starts by exploring the fundamentals of Bi-based catalysts for electrochemical CO2RR. It then delves into synthetic methods and various advanced Bi-based heterojunction structures. Subsequently, the key performance for electrochemical CO2RR performance of Bi-based heterojunctions are discussed. Finally, it provides an in-depth analysis of existing challenges and the current outlook for this field.

Your introduction section is well-structured and covers the background and significance of the research topic extensively. Nevertheless, the section can be enhanced by streamlining the content and sharpening the focus on the unique contribution of your review. Here are some suggestions to further refine the language and organization of your text:

"Fossil fuel consumption, chiefly coal, oil, and natural gas, has driven industrial progress and global prosperity since the 19th century. Yet, the resultant mass emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) has precipitated a cascade of environmental issues—from disrupting the carbon cycle to accelerating global warming. The atmospheric CO2 concentration has surged from 280 ppm in the late 19th century to over 400 ppm today, with projections warning of a potential rise to 570 ppm by 2100 in the absence of intervention.[1,2] This mounting concern has galvanized the exploration of CO2 conversion into fuels and value-added chemicals, such as carbon monoxide, hydrocarbons, and organic acids. Such endeavors hold the promise of mitigating atmospheric CO2 levels while forging a sustainable, carbon-neutral economy.[3,4]

Among the array of approaches for CO2 conversion—spanning electrocatalytic, photocatalytic, biochemical, and thermochemical methodologies—electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR) holds notable promise. It offers several advantages, including operation under mild conditions, high conversion efficiency, compatibility with renewable energy, and ease of product separation.[5-7] Yet, the path towards optimal electrocatalytic conversion is fraught with challenges such as sluggish reaction kinetics, competition with hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), low selectivity, and material instability under potent electrochemical potentials.[8] These hurdles underscore the imperative for catalysts that are highly active, stable, and selective.

Bismuth (Bi)-based nanomaterials have emerged as formidable contenders in electrocatalysis, owing to their virtues of low toxicity, affordable cost, and inherent selectivity towards CO2RR over HER.[9,10] Bi and its compounds, including Bi metal, oxide, sulfide, and subcarbonate (Bi2O2CO3, BOC), have demonstrated potential in achieving high current densities and selectivity for the electrochemical conversion of CO2.[11] Nonetheless, their susceptibility to oxidation and structural reconstruction during CO2RR poses limitations to their practical application, highlighting the need for innovative design strategies.[12]

Heterojunction catalysts, a recent focus in CO2RR advancements, constitute composite materials exhibiting differentiated chemistry at their interfaces.[13] A Bi-based heterojunction offers synergistic site activity, spontaneous charge transfer, enhanced stability through exterior stabilization, and the ability to tailor structural attributes, collectively optimizing CO2 conversion efficiency.[14] Although Bi-based materials and metal heterogenous catalysts have been reviewed for CO2RR,[15-17] a dedicated analysis of Bi-based heterojunctions remains scarce. Thus, this review aims to iteratively bridge the gap, providing an exhaustive exploration of heterojunction configurations that elevate the CO2RR efficacy of Bi-based catalysts (Figure 2). The discourse progresses from Bi-based catalyst fundamentals through intricate synthesis techniques to advanced heterojunction architectures. We further evaluate their electrochemical CO2RR performance, culminating in a critical assessment of the field's challenges and prospective developments."

In this revision, the language has been optimized for clarity and conciseness, and the text now emphasizes the novelty and utility of focusing solely on Bi-based heterojunctions. This refined introduction sets a clear precedent for the depth and scope of your forthcoming analysis.
